data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A serial earthquake storm (c. 1225-1175 BC) triggered cascading infrastructural failure across palatial systems dependent on centralized storage and record-keeping","mechanism": "Anatolian-Aegean fault systems produced clustered seismic events that sequentially destroyed granaries, archives, and fortified administrative centers; palatial redistribution required intact physical infrastructure, while Egyptian temple networks were geographically dispersed and redundantly stored across multiple nodal points (Thebes, Memphis, Heliopolis, provincial estates)","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper", "Mesopotamia proper", "Cyprus post-1150"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological surveys show no tectonic damage layer at Pylos or Hattusa destruction horizons contemporaneous with abandonment", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian temples show concentrated, not dispersed, grain storage in New Kingdom administrative papyri", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Diverges from climatic monocausality; emphasizes physical infrastructure vulnerability over resource scarcity. Posits Egyptian 'antifragility' as architectural-geographic accident rather than institutional design."}
